title: Netflix sued by Texas AG for alleged surveillance, addictive features
content:
Texas sued Netflix on Monday for allegedly tracking, collecting and profiting from data produced by children and other customers without their knowledge.

Attorney General Ken Paxton sued the streaming giant under Texas laws against deceptive business practices, alleging that Netflix billed itself as a âsafe respiteâ from the surveillance and advertising that characterize other big tech platforms while engaging in similar data harvesting practices. The suit also claims that Netflix deceptively designs its platform to be addictive via features like autoplay, and references separate litigation in which juries found that Facebook and other social media platforms are similarly addictive by design.

title: Fears for plastic treaty talks as Norway reviews UN project funding
content:
Move by largest donor to environment programme poses further uncertainty for already troubled negotiations

The largest donor to the United Nations Environment Programme (Unep) is reviewing its funding to the body before its revised budget on 12 May, triggering concern among member states and NGOs.

The news could carry significance for the already troubled plastic treaty negotiations being overseen by Unep. Since 2022 countries have been struggling to agree on how to deal with the volume of plastics being produced and used, a subject widely acknowledged to be one of the most serious environmental issues of the age, but despite six rounds of talks there has been no agreement in sight.

Continue reading...

title: Israel/OPT: Israelâs systematic destruction of high-rise buildings must be investigated as war crimes of wanton destruction and collective punishment
content:
Israelâs unlawful and wanton destruction of civilian high-rise buildings continues to have devastating consequences for displaced Palestinian families in the occupied Gaza Strip, where reconstruction remains a distant dream amid ongoing genocide and air strikes despite the October 2025 so-called ceasefire, said Amnesty International today.

To illustrate the gravity of the wanton destruction that Israeli forces have wreaked on Gaza, Amnesty International today releases an investigation of the Israeli militaryâs levelling of at least 13 multi-storey residential and commercial buildings across Gaza City between September and October 2025. The organization found that the Israeli military severely damaged and destroyed the high-rise buildings, which housed thousands of people â many of them internally displaced â by dropping multiple bombs on each building after forcing residents to leave with almost no notice, and called them to be  investigated as the war crimes of wanton destruction, collective punishment and direct attacks on civilian objects.

title: Angola: Flood Survivors Living in Precarious Conditions
content:
Click to expand Image A railway bridge damaged by heavy floods near the Benguela station in Angola, April 23, 2026. Â© 2026 Phill Magakoe / AFP via Getty Images

(Johannesburg) â Angolan authorities should carry out an independent and credible investigation into the Cavaco River dike collapse and ensure that internally displaced communities receive immediate, adequate, and transparent assistance, Human Rights Watch said today.

Official figures indicate that heavy rainfall and the collapse of the Cavaco River dike on April 12, 2026, triggered devastating floods in Angolaâs western Benguela province, killing at least 19 people, leaving 31 missing, and affecting more than 8,000 families. At least 3,624 people were rescued, 1,540 homes were destroyed, 3,871 were damaged, and 2,586 remained flooded in the weeks following the disaster.

âThe scale of destruction of the Cavaco River dike collapse in Angola raises serious concerns about the authoritiesâ ability to prevent and respond to foreseeable risks,â said Sheila Nhancale, Afri

title: A message from CIVICUS Secretary General, Mandeep S. Tiwana
content:
Dear CIVICUS members, partners and allies,

At the time of writing, many of us are still reeling from the surprise cancellation of Rights Con, the worldâs premier gathering at the intersection of tech and human rights. Our partner, Access Nowâs statement on what transpired is illuminating.

Notably, we are living in a world of profound global tumult. An axis of unaccountable power is trying hard to reverse a lifetime of civil society gains on peace, justice, equality and sustainability. Yet, it is in times like these that our work to hold the line and defend civic freedoms through citizen action matters.  Authoritarians, populists and oligarchs who rule by diktat want us to believe that they are unstoppable and that their power is boundless. Their greatest weapon is manufactured hopelessness. Our greatest weapon is resistance through an unshakeable belief in our common humanity and refusal to accept the obscene, the bizarre, and the profane as the new normal.

Understanding the political economy of oppression is key. Decades of concerted push to entrench marke

title: LGBTQ Data Rescue Hackathon
content:
These past few weeks have felt like months, and these months have been years. Weâve taken great solace in the company of data nerds interested in preserving and providing access to public federal data. But we know that LGBTQ+ data is disproportionately affected, especially the loss of data on sexual orientation and gender identity. 

If the past few weeks have left you feeling frustrated and ready to take action, we invite you to join us for an LGBTQ+ Data Rescue Hackathon  hosted by the Williams Institute, the Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research (ICPSR), the Data Rescue Project, and the Movement Advancement Project (MAP). Our event will be on May 28, 2026 from 12-1:30pm Eastern (9-10:30am Pacific).

Participants will help identify, document, and rescue publicly available federal datasets that include sexual orientation and gender identity (SOGI) measures. This work will support efforts to track changes to the availability of SOGI-related data and preserve information that i

title: How the World Became a Casino
content:
How did we get to a point where itâs legal for anyone to bet on anything? Be it the results of a baseball game or a land war in Europe, if you have access to a credit card and a computer you can try to predict the outcome of anything thatâs happening in the world and win a little bit of money if youâre right. If we know that gambling can lead to high rates gambling addiction and financial ruin, why does it seem like our culture has suddenly embraced it?

For years, anyone who has reported on our increasing addiction to technology has found their way to Natasha Natasha Dow SchÃỳllâs book Addiction by Design: Machine Gambling in Las Vegas. The book is an ethnography of slot machines. It is based on many interviews with the people who make them and play them, a deep investigation of how they work, and how they fit into the larger context of casinos, Las Vegas, and gambling

title: Record-high military spending could support European economies; however, effort will be required
content:
Military spending is rising rapidly both in Europeâs major economies and in the Baltic States, reaching historically high levels and reshaping its role in the economy.

Latviaâs defence investment is also approaching 5% of gross domestic product (GDP), serving not only to strengthen security but also to promote local industry, innovation, and export capacity. This is particularly important at times when the economy faces various risks, as is currently the case, with the conflict in the Middle East threatening to dampen growth.

Growing by more than a third

Last year, at a summit in The Hague, leaders of NATO member states committed to reaching the allianceâs target within ten years â allocating at least 5% of GDP to defence. Some countries are already approaching this threshold.

EU countries continue to invest substantial amounts in defence â expenditure in this area increased by more than 30% between 2021 and 2025, reaching approximately 381 billion euros last year, or 2.1% of EU GDP, according to data from the European Council. Funding for this sector is also expected

title: How Russia lies about the stolen Ukrainian children
content:
âWe didnât kidnap anyoneâ, Russiaâs then-ambassador to the US, Anatoly Antonov, insisted in 2023. âWe saved these childrenâ. The ambassador was not simply denying the systematic deportation and forcible transfer of Ukrainian children by Russian occupation forces in Ukraine, a crime for which there is a growing body of evidence from international organisations, researchers, and human rights groups. He was using a well-known playbook â an information campaign seeking to paint the crime as humanitarian rescue.

The story of Ukraineâs abducted children is not only about the crime itself. It is also about the parallel campaign to obscure that crime â a sustained effort by the Russian state to manipulate narratives, deny responsibility, and recast deportation as humanitarian action. In Russiaâs war against Ukraine, childhoods have been stolen â but so, too, has the truth.

The scale of the problem

The documented forcible transfers of Ukrainian children began with Russiaâs occupation of Crimea and parts of eastern Ukraine in 2014. Since the full-scale invasion of 2022, the scale of the problem has expanded dramatically. Estimates say that

âIf you canât read English road signs,â Mike Collins, a Republican, posted on Facebook in April, âyou donât belong behind the wheel. Period.â

Collins, the owner of a trucking business and a member of the U.S. House of Representativesâ transportation committee, is one of the loudest champions of the Trump administrationâs effort to revoke licenses from nearly 200,000 noncitizen commercial drivers, including thousands of truckers. title: How Africa Can Escape the Debt Trap
content: Despite being the least-indebted continent, Africa remains stuck in a debt trap. The trap does not reflect the volume of debt African countries have accumulatedâwhich is just 3% of the global totalâbut rather how that debt is structured and perceived, which in turn reflects a flawed global financial architecture.

title: In an ant colony, the queen isnât in charge. So who is?
content:
Imagine trying to build a house without a blueprint, find a shortcut through an unfamiliar city without a map, or govern a large organisation with no leaders and no meetings.

It sounds impossible. Yet tiny-brained ants, working without leaders or blueprints, have been solving problems like these for millions of years â and no, the queen isnât the boss telling them what to do.

By almost any measure, ants are a wildly successful group of animals â thereâs an estimated 20 quadrillion of them on Earth and they thrive on every continent but Antarctica.

How have these minuscule animals managed to take over the world (and our kitchens)? The answer is teamwork.

Bustling colonies

Ants are social animals that live in colonies ranging from a few individuals to vast continent-spanning supercolonies containing billions of ants.

Bustling ant colonies display many of the features we associate with human societies, including:

title: New law to better protect Europe's waters takes effect
content: EU rules that update the lists of pollutants in surface and groundwaters have entered into force today. This will add stricter controls on PFAS, pesticides and pharmaceuticals. The rules also address microplastics and antimicrobial resistance to improve water resilience and cut pollution.

title: Building Local Capacity to Advance Open Research: Insights from Two Years of the DataCite Global Access Fund - DataCite
content:
<html><head></head><body>"DataCite exists to create a world where all research outputs, resources, and activities are openly connected, easy to access, and reusable. By building open, community-driven infrastructure, DataCite supports research organizations of all sizes and across all regions in sharing and connecting their work through persistent identifiers (PIDs) and rich metadata, helping strengthen a global and inclusive research ecosystem.

At the same time, institutions in emerging research communities still face significant challenges. These include limited awareness of PIDs and open research infrastructure, a lack of local systems such as established data repositories, financial constraints, technical skill gaps, and language barriers â all of which can make it harder for communities to access and benefit from open infrastructure. These challenges highlight the need for tailored local engagement and outreach.

To help address these barriers, DataCiteâs Global Access Fund (GAF) was launched in 2023 with initial support from the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative. The GAF program provides grants to organizations across Africa, Latin America, Asia, and the Middle East </body></html>

title: Who is jumping, Jack?
content:
Who is jumping, Jack? ist eine interaktive kinetische Installation, die die Armbewegungen der Besucher*innen in mechanische AblÃĊufe umwandelt. Die Bewegungen werden in Echtzeit erfasst und an servogesteuerte Korkenzieher gesendet. Dadurch entsteht eine unmittelbare physische Reaktion auf die Handlung der Besucher*innen.

Unter der spielerischen OberflÃĊche zeigen sich Wiederholung, Disziplin und Kontrolle â Bewegungen werden Teil eines Systems, in dem Rollen verteilt und FÃĊden unsichtbar gezogen werden. Wer ist der Hampelmann, wer der Strippenzieher?

TIME OUT 14, a collaboration between Ars Electronica and the Department of Time-based and Interactive Media Arts at the University of Arts Linz.

title: Nearly 50 Years Later, WKRP in Cincinnati Becomes a Real Radio Station
content:
It took nearly 50 years. WKRP in Cincinnati is no longer just a TV sitcom. Itâs now a real radio station in Cincinnati.

A Cincy-area FM station, known as âThe Oasis,â has adopted the WKRP call letters after acquiring them from a nonprofit radio station in North Carolina. The Raleigh-based station put the call letters up for auction as part of a fundraising effort. And then The Oasis snapped them up.

To mark the official launch last week, the station played the TV showâs theme song for six straight hours. Moving forward, the station will continue playing classic rock from the â60s through the â80s â much like the music featured on the 1978â82 sitcom. title: Upcoming event: Copyright Liability and the Knowledge Ecosystem
content:
On 1 June at 14:00 CEST, COMMUNIA will host an in-person event at TownHall Europe in Brussels exploring one of the most pressing challenges facing Europeâs knowledge ecosystem: how legal uncertainty around copyright affects research, libraries, and public-interest AI development.

What to expect?

Across Europe, universities, research organisations, libraries, and archives rely on copyrighted materials every day to fulfil their public-interest missions. While EU copyright law enables some of these activities (including through exceptions), significant uncertainty remains â particularly in cross-border contexts and emerging AI use cases.

For public-interest actors, this uncertainty is not merely theoretical. Unclear liability rules and legal risk can discourage collaboration and innovation, pushing institutions and public-interest technology developers toward overly cautious approaches.

This event will bring together policymakers, researchers, and practitioners affected by this uncertainty to discuss practical and legal pathways to reduce copyright-related

title: UN Official Would Reject US Funds with Discriminatory Conditions
content:
Click to expand Image Tom Fletcher, UN Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator, during a press conference in Geneva, August 22, 2025. Â© 2025 Salvatore Di Nolfi/Keystone via AP Photo

The senior United Nations humanitarian official said he would refuse US government financial contributions that would require recipients to comply with discriminatory US policies that undermine human rights. 

âThe question is, should we take money under those conditions, knowing that it will save millions of lives, or not?â said Tom Fletcher, head of the UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA). âIâm in the situation where Iâm saying I cannot take that money under those conditions.â

Fletcherâs remarks, delivered in April, were first reported last week by Devex.

Fletcher was referring to conditions that could apply to future US funding due to recent expansion of the USâ Mexico City Policy, which restricts abortion care in foreign aid programs. Known as the âglobal gag rule,â

title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain
content:
A few years ago, while I was covering the rise of AI slop on Facebook, I asked my friends and family if they were getting AI spam fed into their timelines and if they could send me examples. A handful of them responded, sending me obviously AI-generated science fiction scenescapes, shrimp Jesus, and forlorn, starving children begging for sympathy. But a few of my friends sent me images that they thought were AI but were not. Their mental guard was up to the point where they were looking at human-made art and photos and thought it safer to dismiss them as AI rather than be fooled by it.

To browse the internet today, to consume any sort of content at all, is to be bombarded with AI of all sorts. People think things that are fake are real, things that are real are fake. Much has been written about âAI psychosis<

title: Despite Court Order, NYPD Failed to Properly Monitor Stop-and-Frisks by Aggressive Unit
content:
More than a decade ago, a federal court found that the New York City Police Department had been unconstitutionally stopping and frisking Black and Hispanic residents. The ruling laid out required fixes, including something quite basic: The NYPD would review officersâ stops to make sure they were legal.

But for most of the past three years the nationâs largest police department failed to do that for a key part of an aggressive and politically connected unit as it stopped New Yorkers.

The lack of court-required review was recently discovered and disclosed by the NYPDâs federal monitor, which oversees the departmentâs compliance with the 2013 stop-and-frisk decision.

In all, more than 2,000 stops werenât properly reviewed, according to data from the monitor.

The failure involved the Community Response Team, or CRT. A ProPublica investigation last year found that the unit had often sid

title: We found hundreds of huge ancient mass graves hidden in the Sahara desert
content:
We have been on a years-long campaign of satellite remote sensing of the vast desert landscapes in Eastern Sudan.

This involved using satellite aerial imagery to systematically and painstakingly search for archaeological features in Atbai Desert of Eastern Sudan, a small part of the much larger Sahara.

Our team â which includes archaeologists from Macquarie University, Franceâs HiSoMA research unit, and the Polish Academy of Sciences â wanted to tell the story of this desert region between the Nile and the Red Sea, without having to excavate.

One mysterious archaeological feature stood out. We kept finding large, circular mass graves filled with the bones of people and animals, often carefully arranged around a key person at the centre.

Likely built around the fourth and third millennia BCE, all these âenclosure burialâ monuments have a large round enclosure wall, some up to 80 metres in diameter, with humans and their cattle, sheep and goats buried inside.

Our new

title: Forget the AI job apocalypse. AIâs real threat is worker control and surveillance
content:
A new divide is emerging: between workers who use AI at work and those who are managed by it

The real danger that artificial intelligence poses to work is not just job loss â it is the growing divide between people who use AI to extend their skills and those whose working lives are increasingly shaped by opaque, AI-powered systems of surveillance and control.

The debate about artificial intelligence and how it will affect workers is stuck in the wrong place. On one side are warnings that machines are coming for millions of jobs. On the other are claims that AI will turbocharge productivity. Both stories miss what is already happening in workplaces across the world, from Britain to Kenya to the Un
snippet: A new divide is emerging: between workers who use AI at work and those who are managed by itThe real danger that artificial intelligence poses to work is not just job loss â it is the growing divide b

title: City Soundscape
content:

Diese FlÃĊchen sind von einem schwarzen, 3D-gedruckten Rahmen umschlossen, sechs dazwischenliegende quadratische Segmente agieren als Verbindungselemente. Jede hexagonale Seite ist von den architektonischen oder materiellen Gegebenheiten ihres jeweiligen Ortes abgeleitet und uÌbertrÃĊgt rÃĊumliche Merkmale in eine taktile und visuelle Sprache.
Das Werk lÃĊdt zur Interaktion ein: Indem Betrachtende sich nÃĊhern und ihre HÃĊnde auf die OberflÃĊchen legen, kÃ¶nnen sie die darin eingebettete akustische Dimension tiefergehend erkunden. Die Feldaufnahmen â jeweils vor Ort entstanden â reagieren dynamisch auf NÃĊhe und BeruÌhrung und werden lauter, je stÃĊrker die Interaktion ist. MÃ¶glich wird dies durch kapazitive BeruÌhrungssensoren, die die LeitfÃĊhigkeit des menschlichen KÃ¶rpers erfassen.

title: Why Estonia is gambling on AI in schools
content:
Estonia is betting that the future of education wonât be built by keeping artificial intelligence out of classrooms â but by putting it at the center of them.

Rather than trying to stop students, Estonian Education and Research Minister Kristina Kallas told POLITICO that schools should train them to use generative AI to their educational advantage.

âIf you regulate AI out of school, youâre risking significant cognitive decline because the students will be using it anyway,â Kallas said.

That philosophy underpins one of Europeâs most ambitious national experiments in AI-assisted education: A partnership between Tallinn and Sam Altmanâs OpenAI to roll out a customized educational artificial intelligence platform across Estoniaâs upper secondary schools.

Estonia is an eager AI adopter. According to Eurostat, 23.4 percent of the countryâs companies incorporated artificial intelligence in 2025, above the EU average of 20 percent. Tallinn has also not been shy about betting on emerging technologies: it is the birthplace of the once-mighty telecoms app Skype, and h

title: Tunisia: End Abusive Prosecution of Refugee Aid Workers
content:
Click to expand Image Mustapha Djemali. Â© Private

(Beirut) â Five employees of the Tunisian Council for Refugees will stand trial on appeal on May 13, 2026, for their work assisting asylum seekers and refugees, Human Rights Watch said today. The Tunisian authorities should end the abusive prosecution of the employees, compensate them for their unlawful detention, and stop the widespread crackdown on civil society groups. 

Tunisian authorities shut down the Tunisian Council for Refugees in May 2024, arrested its founder and director, Mustapha Djemali, and program manager, Abderrazek Krimi, and prosecuted them alongside four other employees. On November 24, 2025, a Tunis Court of First Instance sentenced Djemali and Krimi to two years in prison, suspending six months and releasing them the same day for time served. It acquitted three other defendants, while the fourth is in a separate judicial proceeding. Djemali and Krimi both appealed the decision, as did the prosecution.

title: Students Boo Commencement Speaker After She Calls AI the âNext Industrial Revolutionâ
content:
Speaking to graduates of University of Central Floridaâs College of Arts and Humanities and Nicholson School of Communication and Media on May 8, commencement speaker Gloria Caulfield, vice president of strategic alliances at Tavistock Group, told graduating humanities students that AI is the ânext industrial revolution,â and was met with thousands of booing graduates.

âAnd letâs face it, change can be daunting. The rise of artificial intelligence is the next industrial revolution,â Caulfield said. At that point, murmurs rippled through the crowd. Caulfield paused, and the crowd erupted into boos. âOh, what happened?â Caulfield said, turning around with her hands out. âOkay, I struck a chord. May I finish?â Someone in the crowd yelled, âAI SUCKS!â

title: Conspiracy theories: do 300,000 Kiwis really believe Canada is building an army of mutant super-raccoons?
content:
Four percent of Americans â roughly 12 million people â believe that âlizard peopleâ secretly control the Earth. At least, that was the finding of an infamous 2013 public opinion survey.

Do so many people really believe such outlandish claims? Or do results like these partly reflect people giving silly answers or deliberately skewing surveys for fun?

US psychiatrist Alexander Scott believes the latter plays a significant role.

Using the survey as an example, he coined the term âthe Lizardman constantâ to describe the idea that a certain amount of noise and trolling will always exist in surveys about unusual beliefs.

title: New law to better protect Europe's waters takes effect
content: EU rules that update the lists of pollutants in surface and groundwaters have entered into force today. This will add stricter controls on PFAS, pesticides and pharmaceuticals. The rules also address microplastics and antimicrobial resistance to improve water resilience and cut pollution.

title: Supporting library workflows: MARC records for our books available from Thoth | University of London Press
content:
Good metadata travels! We have posted previously about working with Thoth. Thoth Open Metadata is a non-profit, open infrastructure platform for publishing, bringing together metadata management, dissemination, hosting, and usage insights in one place. Built for open access books and chapters, Thoth helps publishers, libraries, and platforms manage their metadata once, distribute it widely, host catalogues and websites, and understand how their content is used.

Weâre pleased to have our open access books available via Thoth, because the platform allows publishers to create, manage, and distribute high-quality metadata â including MARC21 and MARCXML records â which libraries can easily download and integrate into their catalogues, either directly from the Thoth Catalogue or via Thothâs Export API. All records are released under a Creative Commons CC0 public domain dedication.

By using Thoth, libraries can streamline cataloguing workflows, reduce manual data entry, and ensure consistent, up-to-date bibliographic records for open access content.

Our full catalogue can be downloaded in MARC formats ...

title: OpenAI offers EU access to new AI hacking model
content:
BRUSSELS â ChatGPT maker OpenAI is in talks with the European Commission to grant EU authorities access to a model capable of identifying software vulnerabilities.

The unexpected move is a boon for Europe after weeks of struggling to grasp the cybersecurity risks coming from novel AI models. But itâs also a stark contrast with OpenAI rival Anthropic, which hasnât yet allowed the EU access to its cybersavvy model Mythos.

OpenAIâs lead executive on the initiative, former U.K. Chancellor of the Exchequer George Osborne, wrote to the Commission in the night from Sunday to Monday to offer access to the advanced AI model capable of exploiting software gaps, he told POLITICO. The company has also kicked off the âprocess of contacting member states,â Osborne added.

âThe idea is âḊ to work with them to make sure that they are probably defended âḊ the institutions, the utilities, the important infrastructure components of Europe,â Osborne said in the exclusive interview.

The move right away eased nerves in Brussels, where cybersecurity and AI officials have fai

title: The womenâs rights crisis in Afghanistan is an ongoing humanitarian calamity
content:
Where is one of worst places to be a woman? Afghanistan.

Thatâs what most people think when it comes to the topic of the womenâs rights crisis under the ruling Taliban in Afghanistan. But this only tells part of the story.

Focusing on the word ârightsâ hides something more serious underneath: how people live and survive in this situation. Whatâs unfolding in Afghanistan is not just a womenâs rights crisis, but a humanitarian disaster.

It affects how people access health care, education, food systems and basic supports and whether these system can function at all when half the population has been systematically removed from them. It forces families to deal with womenâs limited access to work and services, often pushing households into deeper economic and social vulnerability.

The Taliban has steadily removed women from public spaces including
